news_summary: Octopuses have demonstrated remarkable problem-solving skills by using mirrors to locate hidden food.
news_summary_fr: Les pieuvres ont fait preuve d'une remarquable capacité à résoudre des problèmes en utilisant des miroirs pour localiser de la nourriture cachée.
news_summary_es: Los pulpos han demostrado una notable capacidad para resolver problemas al utilizar espejos para localizar comida escondida.

articles.title: Octopuses use mirrors to find food they cannot see
articles.summary: Octopuses may be even smarter than we thought. Researchers at Dartmouth found that octopuses can learn to use mirrors to locate food hidden behind them—a skill previously seen only in vertebrates like mammals and birds. After training, the animals correctly identified the food’s location about 73% of the time, showing they could use a mirror as a tool rather than simply reacting to a reflection.
articles.content:
Octopuses have long been known for their remarkable intelligence. One famous example was Inky, the octopus that escaped from New Zealand's National Aquarium in 2016 by squeezing through a drainpipe and making its way back to the ocean.

Now, researchers at Dartmouth have uncovered another impressive ability. A new study published in Current Biology found that octopuses can learn to use mirrors to locate food hidden from direct view, demonstrating sophisticated spatial thinking.

"Our findings are the first to demonstrate that invertebrates can use mirrors to understand their environment to find prey," says lead author Mary Kieseler, Guarini '25, who conducted the research as a PhD student in the Department of Psychological and Brain Sciences at Dartmouth and is now a postdoc at Switzerland's University of Fribourg. "It's a skill that previously has only been documented in vertebrates, such as in some mammals and some birds."

Octopuses Learn to Use Mirrors

The research team worked with three California two-spot octopuses (Octopus bimaculoides) housed in Dartmouth's Octopus Lab.

Their goal was to determine whether the animals could learn to use a mirror to identify the location of a food source that was out of sight. Instead of attacking the reflected image, the octopuses needed to figure out where the stimulus was actually located and move toward it.

The animals were first given time to become familiar with a mirror placed in their habitat. Next, researchers trained them to understand the relationship between a reflection and the real world. During this phase, a live crab was placed inside a glass jar positioned so the octopus could see it only through the mirror. To reach the crab, the animal had to turn 90 degrees and move around a corner.

"We don't enter the world knowing how to use a mirror but learn how to use a mirror," says senior author and cognitive neuroscientist Peter Tse, a professor of psychological and brain sciences at Dartmouth. Just as new drivers learn to use a rearview mirror to track other vehicles, "Octopuses can also learn how to use a mirror to infer where things are in the world."

Testing Spatial Cognition

Octopuses possess chemoreceptors that allow them to smell and taste through touch, which could have affected the results if real prey had been used during testing. To avoid that issue, the researchers relied on a virtual crab image instead.

For the experiment, each octopus was placed inside a start box that was open at the front and top. A mirror was positioned directly in front of the animal. The virtual crab image appeared behind the octopus, either on its left or right side, but was visible only through the mirror.

To earn a reward, the octopus had to recognize where the image was actually located and move toward that position. Instead of approaching the mirror itself, the animals turned around and headed toward the correct side, where they received a live crab reward. Some octopuses even climbed over the side of the box to reach the location of the projected image rather than swimming around it.

The animals chose the correct side about 73% of the time.

Researchers tracked a point between the octopus's eyes on the mantle, the part of the body comparable to a head, using overhead observations. They also measured the routes the animals took while seeking the reward. Although the octopuses did not always choose the shortest path, they became faster at reaching the correct location as the trials progressed.

Clues About the Evolution of Intelligence

According to the researchers, the findings may offer new insights into how intelligence evolves.

"Octopuses are among the most evolutionarily distant animals from humans, as our last common ancestor was a worm that lived 350 to 500 million years ago," says Kieseler. "Given that such a remote organism has independently evolved the means to use a mirror as a tool to process spatial cognition suggests that the underlying cognitive processes might be subject to convergent evolution, where different species evolve similar neural solutions to the same challenge."

The environments where octopuses live, including coral reefs and the seafloor, are often highly complex and filled with obstacles.

"Octopuses are like cats: they will sneak up on their prey and pounce, and they want to do so as fast as possible, so that they don't become preyed upon," Tse says.

Researchers believe this kind of hunting strategy may benefit from an internal understanding of the surrounding environment.

"Hunters are very effective when they have a mental map of their territory, so that they know where they are in relation to their environments," says Tse. "Our work suggests that octopuses might also have internal maps, an internal representation of space."

The team emphasizes that more research will be needed to determine whether octopuses truly maintain such mental maps. Even so, the study adds another remarkable skill to the growing list of abilities that make octopuses some of the most fascinating animals in the ocean.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about octopus intelligence, showing they can use mirrors as tools to locate hidden food, a cognitive skill previously documented only in vertebrates. This finding has broad implications for understanding animal cognition and the evolution of intelligence, offering inspiring insights into the capabilities of a distant invertebrate species. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful advancement in biological and cognitive science.
articles.personality_title: Octopuses learn to use mirrors to find hidden food
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Octopuses are known for being very smart animals. Scientists have studied them a lot because they can solve problems and escape tricky situations, like Inky, an octopus that escaped from an aquarium in New Zealand in 2016.

**What happened** – Researchers at Dartmouth University trained three California two-spot octopuses to use mirrors to find food that they could not see directly. The food was hidden behind them, and the octopuses had to look in the mirror to know where to go. After practice, the octopuses found the food about 73% of the time by using the mirror as a tool.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have seen an invertebrate (an animal without a backbone) use mirrors to understand its surroundings and find food. Before, this skill was only seen in animals like mammals and birds. It shows that octopuses can think in complex ways and may have mental maps of their environment, helping them hunt and survive.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to learn more about how octopuses think and if they really have mental maps of their space. This could help us understand how intelligence developed in very different animals over millions of years.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Octopuses can learn to use mirrors to find hidden food, showing surprising intelligence usually seen only in animals with backbones.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les pieuvres apprennent à utiliser les miroirs pour trouver de la nourriture cachée
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les pieuvres sont connues pour être des animaux très intelligents. Les scientifiques les étudient beaucoup car elles peuvent résoudre des problèmes et s'échapper de situations compliquées, comme Inky, une pieuvre qui s'est échappée d'un aquarium en Nouvelle-Zélande en 2016.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'université de Dartmouth ont entraîné trois pieuvres californiennes à deux taches à utiliser des miroirs pour trouver de la nourriture qu'elles ne pouvaient pas voir directement. La nourriture était cachée derrière elles, et les pieuvres devaient regarder dans le miroir pour savoir où aller. Après l'entraînement, elles ont trouvé la nourriture environ 73 % du temps en utilisant le miroir comme un outil.

**Impact** – C'est la première fois que les scientifiques observent un invertébré (un animal sans colonne vertébrale) utiliser un miroir pour comprendre son environnement et trouver de la nourriture. Avant, cette capacité n'était observée que chez des animaux comme les mammifères et les oiseaux. Cela montre que les pieuvres peuvent penser de manière complexe et qu'elles pourraient avoir des cartes mentales de leur environnement, ce qui les aide à chasser et à survivre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent en savoir plus sur la façon dont les pieuvres pensent et si elles ont vraiment des cartes mentales de leur espace. Cela pourrait aider à comprendre comment l'intelligence s'est développée chez des animaux très différents au cours de millions d'années.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les pieuvres peuvent apprendre à utiliser des miroirs pour trouver de la nourriture cachée, montrant une intelligence surprenante généralement observée uniquement chez les animaux avec une colonne vertébrale.
articles.personality_title_es: Los pulpos aprenden a usar espejos para encontrar comida oculta
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los pulpos son conocidos por ser animales muy inteligentes. Los científicos los han estudiado mucho porque pueden resolver problemas y escapar de situaciones difíciles, como Inky, un pulpo que escapó de un acuario en Nueva Zelanda en 2016.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Dartmouth entrenaron a tres pulpos californianos de dos puntos para usar espejos y encontrar comida que no podían ver directamente. La comida estaba escondida detrás de ellos, y los pulpos tenían que mirar en el espejo para saber a dónde ir. Después de practicar, encontraron la comida aproximadamente el 73% de las veces usando el espejo como una herramienta.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera vez que los científicos ven que un invertebrado (un animal sin columna vertebral) usa espejos para entender su entorno y encontrar comida. Antes, esta habilidad solo se había visto en animales como mamíferos y aves. Esto muestra que los pulpos pueden pensar de formas complejas y podrían tener mapas mentales de su entorno, lo que les ayuda a cazar y sobrevivir.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren aprender más sobre cómo piensan los pulpos y si realmente tienen mapas mentales de su espacio. Esto podría ayudar a entender cómo evolucionó la inteligencia en animales muy diferentes durante millones de años.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los pulpos pueden aprender a usar espejos para encontrar comida oculta, mostrando una inteligencia sorprendente que normalmente solo se ve en animales con columna vertebral.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a curious California two-spot octopus inside a softly lit underwater den, attentively observing its own reflection in a gently rippling mirror propped against the coral, while a faint, ghostly image of a crab appears just beyond the octopus’s line of sight, symbolizing the octopus’s learned ability to use the mirror to locate hidden food.
